ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 represents Atlantic salmon as a food item; no specific fillet preparation code exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers raw Atlantic salmon; no specific grilled/baked fillet preparation code available; closest_parent used for raw salmon.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227037002 represents salmon as a substance; no fillet-specific preparation code available.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of a salmon fillet is approximately 180 g, representing one boneless fillet portion as commonly plated in Turkish and Mediterranean cuisine restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, salmon, Atlantic, farmed, cooked, dry heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15237)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for Atlantic salmon, farmed, cooked by dry heat used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent cooked salmon fillet per 100 g, which is the most appropriate match for a restaurant-served grilled or baked salmon fillet. Minor seasoning (salt, herbs, olive oil) typical of Turkish preparation may marginally affect sodium; core macros are from the USDA cooked salmon entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure protein and fat foods such as fish fillets contain negligible digestible carbohydrate and are universally assigned an effective GI of 0 in the University of Sydney GI Database framework and peer-reviewed GI literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Carbohydrate_g per 100 g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy), therefore glycemic load per serving is 0.0. Per the Veritas zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule (which applies to all foods with effectively zero glycemic carbohydrates), glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) and gi_category is low per convention.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content and high potassium-to-sodium ratio
hypertension.guidance: Salmon fillet is naturally low in sodium (~59 mg/100 g) and rich in potassium (~363 mg/100 g), making it a favourable protein choice for individuals managing hypertension; however, patients should request minimal added salt during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Effectively zero glycemic carbohydrate content and high-quality protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Salmon fillet contains no digestible carbohydrate and has a glycemic index of 1 (effectively zero), posing no postprandial glucose burden; its high protein and omega-3 fatty acid content may support insulin sensitivity, making it an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid content (EPA and DHA)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salmon fillet is one of the richest dietary sources of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are associated with reduced triglycerides, anti-inflammatory effects, and reduced cardiovascular event risk; regular consumption is consistent with cardioprotective dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salmon fillet is a boneless cut of salmon — most commonly Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar) — that has been trimmed of bones and typically served as a single restaurant portion of approximately 150–200 g. In Turkish and Mediterranean cuisine, salmon fillet is commonly prepared by grilling, baking, or pan-searing with olive oil, lemon juice, fresh dill, parsley, salt, and black pepper, reflecting the clean, herb-forward flavour profile characteristic of Turkish seafood cooking. The flesh is tender, moist, and flaky with a rich, buttery flavour and a distinctive orange-pink colour derived from astaxanthin. Nutritionally, salmon fillet is one of the most nutrient-dense animal proteins available: it provides approximately 20 g of high-quality complete protein per 100 g, 13 g of total fat dominated by heart-healthy monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fatty acids including EPA and DHA omega-3s, and virtually zero carbohydrate. It is an exceptional source of vitamin D (~11 mcg/100 g), vitamin B12 (~3.2 mcg/100 g), selenium (~36.5 mcg/100 g), and niacin. Clinically, salmon fillet is highly recommended for individuals managing cardiovascular disease, Type 2 Diabetes, and hypertension due to its omega-3 content, low sodium, effectively zero glycemic load (GI of 1), and anti-inflammatory properties. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. In Singapore, Turkish salmon fillet dishes are served at restaurants such as Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine, typically priced around SGD 32.90 per portion.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a salmon fillet?
Is salmon fillet good for diabetes?
Is salmon fillet high in omega-3?
Can I eat salmon fillet if I have high blood pressure?
What is the protein content of salmon fillet?
How is Turkish salmon fillet prepared?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for Atlantic salmon, farmed, cooked by dry heat — the closest approved source match for a restaurant-served grilled or baked salmon fillet. GI of 1 (effectively zero) is universally established for non-carbohydrate fish foods in peer-reviewed GI literature and aligns with Veritas rules for zero-glycemic foods. Ontology codes are real and defensible. Multilingual names are accurate.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked Atlantic salmon fillet — salmon contains no digestible carbohydrate by nature (naturally zero). Sugar_g is naturally zero as salmon contains no sugars. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as salmon is an animal product containing no plant fibre. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for farmed Atlantic salmon cooked by dry heat. Glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this food contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category is low per established convention for non-carbohydrate protein foods and Veritas zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule. Glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero). Sodium value (~59 mg/100 g) reflects cooked salmon without heavy added salt; restaurant preparation with seasoning may increase sodium modestly. Vitamin_d_mcg of 11.1 is from USDA SR Legacy and represents one of the highest natural dietary sources of vitamin D. All -1 sentinel values represent micronutrients not separately reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy entry used; however, most key micronutrients for salmon are reported and populated. FoodOn match_type is closest_parent as no specific fillet preparation code exists. FoodEx2 match_type is closest_parent as A00LC covers raw Atlantic salmon without a specific cooked fillet code. SNOMED CT match_type is closest_parent as 227037002 covers salmon as a substance without preparation specificity. Price data from field survey data (SGD 32.90) is preserved as contextual metadata only and not used as a nutritional source.
